items.name: Lead Role Gender Gap
items.definition: Men are cast in lead/protagonist roles far more often than women.
items.example: An action catalog where most protagonists are male.
items.why_it_matters: Leads drive the story; whoever holds them defines whose perspective audiences inhabit.

items.name: Dialogue Gap
items.definition: Female characters receive systematically fewer spoken words than male characters.
items.example: Script analyses repeatedly find male characters speak the majority of lines even in female-led films.
items.why_it_matters: Speech is narrative power: fewer words means less agency and influence on the plot.

items.name: Smurfette Principle
items.definition: A single female character in an otherwise all-male ensemble.
items.example: A five-person team with exactly one woman whose trait is 'the girl'.
items.why_it_matters: Frames womanhood itself as a personality trait rather than half the population.

items.name: Bechdel Test Failure
items.definition: No two named women converse about something other than a man.
items.example: Entire feature films where women never speak to each other.
items.why_it_matters: A deliberately low bar — failing it signals women exist only relative to men.

items.name: Fridging
items.definition: Harming or killing a female character solely to motivate a male protagonist.
items.example: The hero's wife dies in the opening scene to justify his revenge arc.
items.why_it_matters: Reduces women to plot devices instead of people with their own arcs.

items.name: Male Gaze Framing
items.definition: Camera work and framing that presents women primarily as objects to be looked at.
items.example: Introductory shots that pan over a woman's body before showing her face.
items.why_it_matters: Trains audiences to evaluate women by appearance before character.

items.name: Romantic-Interest Typecasting
items.definition: Women cast disproportionately as love interests rather than agents of the plot.
items.example: A scientist character whose only scenes concern her relationship with the hero.
items.why_it_matters: Limits the imagined futures of audiences who see themselves only as someone's partner.

items.name: Ambition Penalty
items.definition: Ambitious female characters written as villains or punished by the narrative.
items.example: The career-driven woman who 'learns her lesson' and gives it all up.
items.why_it_matters: Codes ambition as masculine and punishes audiences who aspire.

items.name: STEM Role Gap
items.definition: Scientists, engineers and tech experts on screen are overwhelmingly male.
items.example: Hacker and genius-inventor roles defaulting to men.
items.why_it_matters: On-screen role models shape real-world career aspiration, especially in childhood.

items.name: Romantic Age-Gap Casting
items.definition: Male leads paired with much younger female love interests, rarely the reverse.
items.example: A 50-year-old lead whose romantic partner is 25.
items.why_it_matters: Normalizes valuing women by youth while letting men age into prestige.

items.name: Non-binary Erasure
items.definition: Non-binary people are nearly absent from mainstream casts.
items.example: Catalogs with thousands of characters and only a handful outside the gender binary.
items.why_it_matters: Invisibility tells real people their existence is not part of the shared story.

items.name: Racial Lead Gap
items.definition: Leads are disproportionately white relative to the population.
items.example: A platform's top-billed cast skewing heavily white year after year.
items.why_it_matters: Decides whose stories are treated as universal and whose as niche.

items.name: Tokenism
items.definition: A single minority character included to signal diversity without depth.
items.example: The one Black friend with no storyline of their own.
items.why_it_matters: Substitutes optics for actual representation and flattens identity into a checkbox.

items.name: Whitewashing
items.definition: Casting white actors in roles written as people of color.
items.example: A film adaptation recasting an explicitly Asian protagonist as white.
items.why_it_matters: Erases the few roles explicitly written for underrepresented actors.

items.name: Racial Dialogue Gap
items.definition: Characters of color receive fewer words than white characters with similar billing.
items.example: A diverse ensemble where white characters still dominate the script.
items.why_it_matters: Casting diversity is hollow if the script gives the words to the same group.

items.name: Ethnic Villain Coding
items.definition: Villains given foreign accents or ethnic coding while heroes stay local.
items.example: Action franchises where antagonists default to a foreign accent.
items.why_it_matters: Builds an instinctive link between 'foreign' and 'threat'.

items.name: Model Minority Trope
items.definition: Asian characters confined to studious, quiet, technical archetypes.
items.example: The Asian classmate who exists to be good at math.
items.why_it_matters: Even 'positive' stereotypes deny individuality and create impossible standards.

items.name: Magical Minority Trope
items.definition: Minority characters who exist to spiritually guide a white protagonist.
items.example: The wise Black mentor with no inner life who dies for the hero's growth.
items.why_it_matters: Centers white narratives even inside roles given to people of color.

items.name: Accent Caricature
items.definition: Exaggerated accents played for comedy.
items.example: A South Asian character whose every line is an accent joke.
items.why_it_matters: Turns identity into a punchline that follows real people into daily life.

items.name: Colorism
items.definition: Lighter-skinned actors favored for leading and romantic roles.
items.example: Darker-skinned actors cast as villains or background while leads stay light-skinned.
items.why_it_matters: Adds a second hierarchy inside already underrepresented groups.

items.name: Racial Screen-Time Gap
items.definition: Characters of color get fewer minutes on screen even when cast.
items.example: Poster-prominent minority characters with minimal actual scenes.
items.why_it_matters: Marketing diversity that the runtime doesn't deliver is a bait-and-switch.

items.name: Ethnic Interchangeability
items.definition: Treating distinct ethnicities as interchangeable in casting.
items.example: Casting any East Asian actor for any East Asian nationality.
items.why_it_matters: Signals that specific cultures don't matter, only a generic 'look'.

items.name: Casting Age Cliff
items.definition: Roles for women drop sharply after 40 while men keep leading into their 60s.
items.example: Award-winning actresses reporting role droughts at exactly the age male peers peak.
items.why_it_matters: Halves the careers of one gender and erases older women from public imagination.

items.name: Elder Invisibility
items.definition: Characters over 50 are heavily underrepresented relative to the population.
items.example: Casts where over-50s are a small fraction despite being a third of viewers.
items.why_it_matters: An aging audience never sees its own present, only its past.

items.name: Grumpy Elder Trope
items.definition: Older characters reduced to stubborn, technophobic comic relief.
items.example: The grandparent whose jokes are all about not understanding phones.
items.why_it_matters: Replaces the diversity of late life with a single condescending gag.

items.name: Adult-as-Teen Casting
items.definition: Casting late-20s actors as teenagers.
items.example: High-school dramas cast almost entirely with adults.
items.why_it_matters: Sets impossible appearance standards for actual teenagers.

items.name: Age Dialogue Gap
items.definition: Older characters speak less than their share of the cast.
items.example: Ensemble shows where over-50 characters get the fewest lines per episode.
items.why_it_matters: Even when present, elders are seen and not heard.

items.name: Sentiment Skew
items.definition: Some demographics are consistently written with more negative emotional tone.
items.example: A group whose lines are disproportionately angry, fearful or sad.
items.why_it_matters: Tone is subtext: it quietly teaches audiences how to feel about a group.

items.name: Competence Framing Gap
items.definition: Identical actions framed as brilliant for one group, lucky for another.
items.example: His risky plan is genius; her identical plan 'somehow works'.
items.why_it_matters: Audiences absorb who is allowed to be skilled.

items.name: Sassy Sidekick Trope
items.definition: Minority characters written as quippy support with no interior life.
items.example: The one-liner best friend who vanishes between the hero's scenes.
items.why_it_matters: Personality without story is decoration, not representation.

items.name: Trauma-Only Storylines
items.definition: Marginalized characters whose plots are exclusively about suffering.
items.example: LGBTQ+ arcs that are only coming-out pain or tragedy.
items.why_it_matters: Joy is part of life; stories that allow a group only pain distort reality.

items.name: Accent-Intelligence Coding
items.definition: Regional or working-class accents written as markers of low intelligence.
items.example: The rural-accented character who exists to misunderstand things.
items.why_it_matters: Maps class prejudice directly onto how people are born speaking.

items.name: Expertise Voice Imbalance
items.definition: Explanations and expertise are voiced through one demographic.
items.example: Documentaries where nearly all expert talking heads are men.
items.why_it_matters: Defines who sounds like authority.

items.name: Named-Role Gap
items.definition: Underrepresented actors more often cast in unnamed background parts.
items.example: Credits listing 'Nurse #2' while speaking roles skew one way.
items.why_it_matters: Names are narrative existence; the unnamed are scenery.

items.name: Bury Your Gays
items.definition: LGBTQ+ characters killed off at disproportionate rates.
items.example: A long pattern of same-sex couples ending in one partner's death.
items.why_it_matters: Teaches queer audiences their stories end badly by default.

items.name: Inspiration Porn
items.definition: Disabled characters existing solely to inspire able-bodied characters.
items.example: The wheelchair-using classmate whose role is making others grateful.
items.why_it_matters: Objectifies disability as a moral prop instead of a lived experience.

items.name: Disability Villain Coding
items.definition: Scars, prosthetics and disfigurement used as shorthand for evil.
items.example: Villains marked by facial scars while heroes are unblemished.
items.why_it_matters: Links visible difference with moral corruption.

items.name: White Savior Arc
items.definition: Stories about marginalized communities centered on a white rescuer.
items.example: A historical drama about racism whose hero is a white ally.
items.why_it_matters: Hands the narrative of a community's struggle to someone outside it.

items.name: Poverty Spectacle
items.definition: Working-class life shown only as misery or comedy for outside audiences.
items.example: Reality formats that frame poor families as entertainment.
items.why_it_matters: Replaces solidarity with spectatorship.

items.name: Redemption Arc Gap
items.definition: Redemption arcs offered to some demographics and denied to others.
items.example: The troubled white antihero is complex; a minority character doing the same is simply a criminal.
items.why_it_matters: Complexity is empathy; rationing it rations audience compassion.

items.name: Heteronormative Default
items.definition: All characters presumed straight unless a plot point says otherwise.
items.example: Large casts with zero incidental queer characters.
items.why_it_matters: Makes a sizable share of the audience a permanent special case.

items.name: Queerbaiting
items.definition: Hinting at queer relationships for engagement without committing on screen.
items.example: Marketing that teases a same-sex pairing the show never acknowledges.
items.why_it_matters: Monetizes a community's hope while avoiding its representation.

items.name: Director Gender Gap
items.definition: Women direct a small fraction of top-budget productions.
items.example: Studio slates where female directors are confined to lower budgets.
items.why_it_matters: Whoever directs decides every other bias on this list.

items.name: Writers' Room Homogeneity
items.definition: Writing staff demographics far narrower than the audience's.
items.example: Shows about diverse cities written by homogeneous rooms.
items.why_it_matters: You can't write lives no one in the room has lived.

items.name: Budget Allocation Gap
items.definition: Projects led by underrepresented creators receive smaller budgets and marketing.
items.example: A diverse-led show canceled for ratings it was never marketed to reach.
items.why_it_matters: Underfunding manufactures the 'failure' used to justify the next underfunding.

items.name: Identity-Mismatch Casting
items.definition: Able-bodied, cis, or hearing actors cast in disabled, trans, or Deaf roles.
items.example: Award-winning performances in roles the community could have played itself.
items.why_it_matters: Takes both the role and the paycheck from the people being depicted.

items.name: Pay Disparity
items.definition: Co-leads of different demographics paid wildly different salaries.
items.example: Reported negotiations where a female co-lead earned a fraction of her partner's fee.
items.why_it_matters: The industry's private valuation of whose presence 'sells'.

items.name: Below-the-Line Gap
items.definition: Cinematography, sound, and technical crews remain heavily homogeneous.
items.example: Camera departments with near-zero female department heads.
items.why_it_matters: Technical craft shapes the image itself — including who is lit and framed well.

items.name: Thumbnail Personalization Bias
items.definition: Recommendation systems can choose artwork that misrepresents diverse casts to different users.
items.example: Artwork emphasizing minor white characters when shown to some users.
items.why_it_matters: Personalization can quietly resegregate the storefront.

items.name: Recommendation Feedback Loop
items.definition: Engagement-trained algorithms amplify whatever was historically promoted.
items.example: Diverse titles surfacing less because they were surfaced less before.
items.why_it_matters: Yesterday's bias becomes tomorrow's 'data-driven' decision.

items.name: Category Ghettoization
items.definition: Diverse titles shelved into niche categories instead of mainstream rows.
items.example: A universal family drama categorized only under a demographic label.
items.why_it_matters: Tells general audiences these stories aren't for them.

items.name: Search Ranking Skew
items.definition: Search and autocomplete favoring established (historically homogeneous) hits.
items.example: Generic queries returning the same legacy catalog first.
items.why_it_matters: Discovery is destiny on streaming platforms.

items.name: Completion-Metric Myopia
items.definition: Judging shows on metrics that penalize culturally specific storytelling.
items.example: Slow-burn international titles canceled on week-one completion rates.
items.why_it_matters: A single metric becomes a quiet censor of storytelling styles.

items.name: Intersectional Lead Gap
items.definition: Women of color underrepresented far beyond what gender or race gaps alone predict.
items.example: Lead shares for women of color far below either women overall or people of color overall.
items.why_it_matters: Bias multiplies at intersections; single-axis fixes miss the people most excluded.

items.name: Gendered Ageism
items.definition: Age penalties land harder on women than men at every age bracket.
items.example: Older male leads paired against a revolving cast of young women.
items.why_it_matters: Two biases compound into one steep cliff.

items.name: Queer POC Underrepresentation
items.definition: LGBTQ+ characters of color are rare even within queer representation.
items.example: Queer storylines defaulting to white characters.
items.why_it_matters: Representation that only reaches a group's most privileged members isn't done.

items.name: Disabled Women of Color Erasure
items.definition: Characters at triple intersections are nearly absent from screens.
items.example: Catalogs with effectively zero disabled women of color in named roles.
items.why_it_matters: Total invisibility is the strongest message a medium can send.
